content.text: Before You Start: What You Need

Creating an AWS account takes 5-10 minutes if you have everything ready. The process involves five stages — account information, contact details, payment method, phone verification, and support plan selection — and requires three things you must have available before you begin:

A valid email address that you control and that receives email reliably. This email becomes the root user login for the account. Recommendation: create a dedicated mailbox for the AWS account (e.g., aws-root@yourcompany.com) rather than using a personal or shared team inbox — the root user email must stay accessible indefinitely and should not change hands when people leave the company.

A credit or debit card (Visa, Mastercard, American Express, or Discover). AWS places a $1 temporary authorization charge to verify the card is valid; this charge reverses within 3-5 business days and does not result in an actual payment. Prepaid debit cards and virtual card numbers are sometimes rejected by the verification system.

A phone number that can receive SMS text messages or automated voice calls. AWS calls or texts this number to verify your identity. VoIP numbers (Google Voice, Skype, TextNow) are sometimes rejected — if you encounter an error, use a mobile phone number.
The Five-Step Sign-Up Process

attrs.alt: AWS sign-up flow: five steps from account info through activation, plus the immediate post-signup security checklist

content.text: The diagram above shows the complete sign-up flow and the three critical security steps to take immediately after account activation. Do not skip the post-signup security steps — a new account with no MFA on the root user and no billing alerts is a security and financial risk.
Step 1: Account Information
Navigate to aws.amazon.com and click 'Create an AWS Account.' You will enter:
Root user email address — this permanently identifies the account; choose carefully
AWS account name — a human-readable label visible on invoices and the console header; can be changed later
Root user password — at least 8 characters with uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols; store this in a password manager

After submitting, AWS sends a verification email to the address you provided. You must click the link in that email before the sign-up can proceed. Check spam if the email does not arrive within 5 minutes.
Step 2: Contact Information
AWS collects your contact details for billing and account recovery. The most important choice on this page is account type: Personal or Business/Professional.
Account Type
Use When
Tax Implications
Personal
Individual experimenting or learning; sole proprietor using personal credit card
AWS collects your personal name for tax forms (1099 in the US if applicable)
Professional / Business
A company, LLC, partnership, or any organization; team using a business card
AWS collects company name, address, and may request tax ID (EIN, VAT, GST depending on country)

You can use either type for technical purposes — the choice primarily affects how billing documents are formatted and whether you can provide a tax-exempt status or VAT number. If in doubt, choose Professional and enter your organization name.
Step 3: Payment Method

Enter a credit or debit card number, expiration date, and billing address. AWS accepts Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and Discover. In some regions, additional local payment methods are available (bank transfers, specific local card networks).

AWS places a $1 USD authorization charge (not an actual charge) when you submit the payment form. This appears as a pending transaction in your bank statement and disappears within 3-5 business days. It is not a fee — it is a verification check that the card can be charged if needed. If you see the charge, you are not being billed for anything; it will reverse automatically.

Common issue: If your card is declined, double-check that the billing address you entered exactly matches what your bank has on file. Address mismatch is the most common reason for payment verification failure during sign-up.
Step 4: Phone Identity Verification

AWS verifies your identity by sending a PIN to your phone via SMS or automated voice call. Select your country, enter your phone number, choose SMS or voice call, and click 'Send SMS' or 'Call me.' You will receive a 4-digit PIN within 30 seconds. Enter the PIN on the website to complete verification.

If the PIN does not arrive within 2 minutes: first check that you entered the number correctly, including country code. Retry with the other verification method (if you tried SMS, try voice call). If problems persist, AWS Support can assist even during account creation — there is a support link on the verification page.
Step 5: Support Plan Selection
The final step is choosing a support plan. AWS offers four tiers:
Plan
Monthly Cost
Response Time
Best For
Basic
Free
Documentation only; no case support
New accounts, learning, experiments
Developer
$29/month or 3% of monthly usage
Business hours; 12h for general questions
Dev/test environments; individual developers
Business
$100/month or 10% of monthly usage
24x7; 1h for production system down
Production workloads; SLA requirements
Enterprise On-Ramp
$5,500/month
24x7; 30min for critical cases
Large businesses; dedicated TAM pool

Select Basic for now. You can upgrade at any time from the AWS Support Center without needing AWS assistance. Most individuals and new projects start on Basic and upgrade when they have production workloads that need guaranteed support response times. Developer and Business plans are month-to-month with no commitment.
Account Activation Timeline
After completing all five steps, AWS activates the account. Most accounts activate within minutes, but AWS states that activation can take up to 24 hours. During the activation period:
You receive a 'Welcome to Amazon Web Services' email confirming your account number (a 12-digit ID) and your root user email
You can log in to the AWS console but some services (particularly EC2) may display an error if you attempt to provision resources before full activation
The AWS Free Tier is immediately available once the account activates
Your account number is permanent and cannot change — save the welcome email

Your 12-digit AWS Account ID is visible in the top-right corner of the console under your account name. You will need this number frequently: for cross-account IAM policies, CloudTrail configuration, Cost Explorer reports, and when contacting AWS Support.
Immediate Post-Sign-Up: Securing the Root User

The single most important thing to do immediately after account activation is to enable multi-factor authentication on the root user. A new AWS account with only a password on the root user is a high-value target — a compromised root user can create thousands of EC2 instances for cryptocurrency mining, add users and exfiltrate data, and close the account entirely. Root user compromise is one of the most common and costly cloud security incidents.
Enabling MFA on the Root User (Console)
Sign in to the AWS console as the root user (using the email and password you created during sign-up)
Click the account name in the top-right corner, then select 'Security credentials'
In the 'Multi-factor authentication (MFA)' section, click 'Assign MFA device'
Choose 'Authenticator app' (TOTP) — Google Authenticator, Authy, and 1Password all work
Scan the QR code with your authenticator app
Enter two consecutive 6-digit codes from the app to confirm the device is correctly configured
Click 'Add MFA' — the device is now registered

After enabling MFA, the next root user login will require both the password and the 6-digit code from your authenticator app. Store the authenticator backup codes in your password manager or a secure location — losing access to both your password and your MFA device means account recovery through AWS Support, which can take days.

# Verify MFA is enabled on the root user via AWS CLI
# (run this after logging in with a non-root IAM user)
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountMFAEnabled'
# Returns 1 if root MFA is enabled, 0 if not

# Check your account's password policy and security status
aws iam generate-credential-report && sleep 5
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | head -20
Creating Your First IAM Admin User

After securing the root user, create an IAM user with administrative permissions and use that for all subsequent work. The root user should be reserved exclusively for the handful of tasks that require it (changing account email, closing the account, modifying support plan).

# Create an IAM user for day-to-day administration
# (run as root user or skip if doing this in console)

# Create the user
aws iam create-user --user-name admin-yourname

# Attach the AdministratorAccess managed policy
aws iam attach-user-policy \
    --user-name admin-yourname \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Create console login credentials (password)
aws iam create-login-profile \
    --user-name admin-yourname \
    --password 'YourSecurePassword123!' \
    --password-reset-required

# Create API access keys for CLI/SDK use
aws iam create-access-key --user-name admin-yourname
# Save the output! SecretAccessKey is shown only once.

# Verify the user and policy attachment
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name admin-yourname

After creating the IAM user, sign out of the root user session and sign in as the new IAM admin user. From this point forward, use the IAM user for all console and CLI work. Keep the root user credentials stored securely and only retrieve them for root-only operations.

Security note: When creating access keys for the admin IAM user, the SecretAccessKey is only shown once in the API response. Copy it immediately and store it securely. If you lose it, you must deactivate and recreate the access key — there is no way to retrieve a secret access key after the initial creation response.
Enabling Billing Access and Cost Alerts

New accounts require an explicit step to enable IAM users to access billing information. Without this step, your IAM admin user will see an 'Access Denied' error when viewing the billing dashboard, even with AdministratorAccess attached.
Enable IAM Billing Access
Sign in as the root user (temporarily)
Click account name → 'Account'
Scroll to 'IAM user and role access to Billing information'
Click 'Edit' and check 'Activate IAM Access'
Click 'Update'
Sign out and return to your IAM user session
Create a Budget Alert
An AWS Budget notifies you via email when your spending crosses a threshold you set. For a new account, a $10/month threshold gives early warning before any meaningful bill accumulates:

The budget above alerts you when actual spending exceeds 80% of the $10 limit (at $8). You can also add a second notification at 100% threshold. AWS Budgets are free for the first two budgets per account.
Configuring the AWS CLI After Sign-Up
Once your IAM user has access keys, configure the AWS CLI to use them. The CLI must be installed separately — it is not part of the AWS account creation process.

# Install AWS CLI (macOS with Homebrew)
brew install awscli

# Install AWS CLI (Linux)
curl 'https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip' -o awscliv2.zip
unzip awscliv2.zip && sudo ./aws/install

# Configure the CLI with your IAM user's access keys
aws configure
# AWS Access Key ID: AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
# AWS Secret Access Key: wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY
# Default region name: us-east-1
# Default output format: json

# Verify it works
aws sts get-caller-identity
# Returns: UserId, Account (12-digit), Arn of the IAM user

The 'Default region name' you set here becomes the region the CLI targets unless you pass --region on each command. Choose the region closest to you geographically or the region where you plan to run most workloads. us-east-1 (Northern Virginia) is the default for many services and is where new features often launch first.
Named Profiles for Multiple Accounts

# Configure a named profile (useful when managing multiple accounts)
aws configure --profile personal
# Prompts for Access Key ID, Secret Key, Region, Output

# Use the named profile for a command
aws s3 ls --profile personal

# Set default profile for a shell session
export AWS_PROFILE=personal
aws s3 ls  # now uses personal profile automatically

# View all configured profiles
aws configure list-profiles

# Check which identity a profile uses
aws sts get-caller-identity --profile personal
Common Sign-Up Issues and Resolutions
Issue
Symptom
Resolution
Credit card declined
Payment verification fails; cannot proceed past billing step
Use a different card; verify billing address matches bank records; avoid prepaid cards
Phone verification fails
PIN code never arrives; verification page shows error
Switch from SMS to voice call; use a mobile number instead of VoIP; try again after 10 minutes
Email verification not received
Verification email never arrives after step 1
Check spam folder; verify email address was entered correctly; resend using the link on the page
Account stuck in pending state
Welcome email received but services unavailable after 24 hours
Contact AWS Support; provide account ID from welcome email
Prepaid card rejected
Payment verification fails even with a valid prepaid Visa
Use a regular credit/debit card linked to a bank account; prepaid and gift cards are not always accepted
Tax form requirements
AWS requests additional tax information after sign-up
In the US: provide SSN (individual) or EIN (business); in EU: provide VAT number; in Australia: provide ABN
Account Limits for New Accounts

New AWS accounts have lower default service quotas than established accounts. AWS applies conservative defaults to prevent fraud and to protect new customers from accidental large-scale deployments. The most impactful limits for new accounts:
EC2 vCPUs: 32 vCPUs for On-Demand Standard instances by default (a single c5.9xlarge uses all 36 vCPUs, exceeding the limit)
EC2 Elastic IP addresses: 5 per region (this limit is easily reached when working with NAT gateways and ELBs)
CloudFormation stacks: 200 per region (rarely hit for new accounts but low compared to established accounts)
Lambda concurrent executions: 1,000 per region
SES sending quota: 200 emails per 24 hours from the sandbox (production limits require a support request)
Service Quotas increases: automatically approved for small increases; may take 24-48 hours for the first request on a new account

If you attempt to launch resources and receive a 'limit exceeded' error within the first hours of sign-up, the account may still be in the process of activating or applying initial quotas. Wait 30 minutes and retry. If limits persist beyond 24 hours, submit a Service Quotas increase request through the Service Quotas console.
The 12-Month Free Tier

Once your account activates, the 12-month Free Tier begins automatically. The clock starts at account creation — there is no way to pause or reset it. The most useful Free Tier services for getting started:
Service
Free Tier Allotment
Practical Meaning
Amazon EC2
750 hours/month of t2.micro or t3.micro (Linux)
Run one small instance continuously all month
Amazon S3
5 GB storage, 20,000 GET requests, 2,000 PUT requests
Store small datasets, static websites, backups
Amazon RDS
750 hours/month of db.t2.micro or db.t3.micro
Run one small database instance continuously
Amazon CloudFront
1 TB data transfer out, 10M HTTP requests
Serve a small static site globally
AWS Lambda
1M requests/month and 400,000 GB-seconds compute
Always free (not limited to 12 months)
Amazon DynamoDB
25 GB storage, 25 RCU, 25 WCU
Always free (not limited to 12 months)

The t2.micro and t3.micro hours are shared across all EC2 instances in your account. If you run two t2.micro instances simultaneously, you use 2 hours of Free Tier per hour (1,500 combined hours, not 750 each). Track your Free Tier usage under Billing → Free Tier to avoid unexpected charges.
Post-Activation Verification Script
After completing sign-up and the initial security setup, run this verification script to confirm your account is correctly configured:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
AWS account post-signup verification.
Run after configuring the AWS CLI with your IAM admin user credentials.
"""
import boto3
import json

def check_account_setup():
    issues = []

    # 1. Verify CLI is configured and can reach AWS
    sts = boto3.client('sts')
    identity = sts.get_caller_identity()
    print(f"Account ID:  {identity['Account']}")
    print(f"IAM User:    {identity['Arn']}")
    print(f"User ID:     {identity['UserId']}")

    # 2. Check root MFA status
    iam = boto3.client('iam')
    summary = iam.get_account_summary()['SummaryMap']
    root_mfa = summary.get('AccountMFAEnabled', 0)
    if root_mfa:
        print("✓ Root user MFA: ENABLED")
    else:
        issues.append("✗ Root user MFA: NOT ENABLED — enable immediately")

    # 3. Check for active root access keys (should have none)
    # Root keys are visible in the credential report
    try:
        iam.generate_credential_report()
    except:
        pass  # Report may already be recent

    import time, base64, csv, io
    time.sleep(3)
    report_b64 = iam.get_credential_report()['Content']
    report_text = base64.b64decode(report_b64).decode()
    reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(report_text))
    for row in reader:
        if row['user'] == '<root_account>':
            if row['access_key_1_active'] == 'true' or row['access_key_2_active'] == 'true':
                issues.append("✗ Root user has active access keys — deactivate immediately")
            else:
                print("✓ Root user access keys: none active (correct)")

    # 4. Check for a budget
    budgets = boto3.client('budgets')
    account_id = identity['Account']
    budget_list = budgets.describe_budgets(AccountId=account_id)['Budgets']
    if budget_list:
        print(f"✓ AWS Budgets: {len(budget_list)} budget(s) configured")
    else:
        issues.append("✗ No AWS Budgets configured — create a spending alert")

    # 5. Check the default region
    session = boto3.session.Session()
    print(f"✓ Default region: {session.region_name}")

    # Report
    if issues:
        print("\n--- ACTION REQUIRED ---")
        for issue in issues:
            print(issue)
    else:
        print("\n✓ All checks passed — account is correctly configured")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    check_account_setup()
Configuring Account Contacts and Alternate Contacts

AWS sends operational notifications — security alerts, billing summaries, and service disruption notices — to the account email by default. Configuring alternate contacts routes these to the right teams instead of a single inbox.
In Account Settings, you can set three alternate contact types:
Billing
(invoice and payment notices),
Operations
(maintenance and event notifications), and
Security
(vulnerability disclosures and IAM findings).
Navigate to the root account → Account → Alternate Contacts section
Enter a dedicated email and phone for each contact type
Use team distribution lists rather than individual addresses to survive personnel changes
Security contact is especially important — AWS will use it if your account is compromised

# Verify alternate contacts are set (requires aws-cli and appropriate permissions)
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type SECURITY
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type BILLING
aws account get-alternate-contact --alternate-contact-type OPERATIONS
The Account API requires the account management service endpoint — ensure your credentials have the aws-account:GetAlternateContact permission.
AWS Organizations: Planning for Multi-Account Growth

Even if you are starting with a single account, understanding AWS Organizations from day one prevents costly restructuring later. Organizations is the service that groups accounts into a hierarchy managed from a management account.
Key concepts: the
management account
(formerly master account) is the root of your organization.
Organizational Units (OUs)
group accounts by environment, team, or compliance boundary.
Service Control Policies (SCPs)
restrict what services member accounts can use, regardless of IAM permissions.
Concern
Single Account
Multi-Account with Organizations
Blast radius
All workloads share one account — one mistake affects everything
Production isolated from dev/test; breach contained to one account
Billing
One bill, no cost allocation between teams
Consolidated billing with per-account cost reports
Compliance
Single compliance boundary for everything
PCI or HIPAA workloads in isolated accounts with restrictive SCPs
Limits
All quotas shared across all workloads
Per-account service quotas; workloads don't compete

Even for personal projects, creating your first account as the management account of an organization (using the free tier of Organizations) gives you a clean foundation to add sandbox or production accounts later.
Understanding Your AWS Account ID
Every AWS account is identified by a 12-digit
Account ID

(e.g., 123456789012). This ID appears in ARNs (Amazon Resource Names), cross-account IAM trust policies, CloudTrail log prefixes, and S3 bucket policies. It is not secret but should not be published unnecessarily.

# Retrieve your account ID at any time:
aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text

# Full caller identity (also shows User/Role ARN):
aws sts get-caller-identity

The account ID is stable and never changes. When sharing resources across accounts — granting a partner account access to an S3 bucket or an SNS topic, for example — the account ID is the principal identifier in the resource policy.
Tag Strategy from Day One

AWS resource tagging is a practice that costs nothing to start and saves significant operational effort as your infrastructure grows. Tags are key-value pairs attached to resources that power cost allocation reports, automation rules, and access policies.
Define a minimal tag schema before you provision your first resource:
Tag Key
Example Values
Purpose
Environment
dev, staging, prod
Separate cost and access by lifecycle stage
Project
website, data-pipeline
Attribute cost to business initiatives
Owner
team-platform, alice@example.com
Identify who to contact about a resource
CostCenter
Feed AWS Cost Explorer reports by business unit
Enable
Cost Allocation Tags
in Billing → Cost allocation tags after applying your first tags. It takes 24 hours for new tags to appear in Cost Explorer. Tags on resources created before activation are not backfilled.
Tip: Use AWS Tag Editor (Resource Groups → Tag Editor) to audit tag coverage across all regions and resource types in your account.
Summary

Creating an AWS account takes 5-10 minutes across five steps: account information (email, name, password), contact details (personal or business type), payment method (card with $1 authorization), phone verification (PIN via SMS or call), and support plan selection (choose Basic for now). Activation completes within minutes in most cases, up to 24 hours at most.

Three immediate actions are required the moment the account activates: enable MFA on the root user (before doing anything else), create an IAM admin user for day-to-day work, and enable billing alerts to catch unexpected spending. New accounts start with conservative service quotas and a 12-month Free Tier that covers basic compute, storage, and database usage. Configure the AWS CLI with IAM user credentials immediately — you will use it constantly for every topic that follows.
